pattern: Reversing the digits just gives the other end of a small, fixed range -- once the range is known, a plain sieve (or trial division, since the range is tiny) picks out and sums the primes inside it.
hints: Reverse the digits of n by converting it to a string, flipping it, and parsing it back to an integer -- this also naturally drops any leading zero the reversal creates.
The two endpoints of the range are simply the smaller and larger of n and its reversal; every integer between them (inclusive) is a candidate.
Since the range never exceeds a few thousand, test each candidate for primality directly (trial division up to its square root) and add up the ones that pass.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, reversal drops a trailing zero
statement example, a palindrome-like case with no primes
a true palindrome, so the range collapses to a single prime value
a true palindrome that is itself composite
reversal produces a much smaller number due to trailing zeros
n itself is the smallest prime

stress.description: random n across the full constraint range
